item.title: 4140 vs 4340 Steel: When Does the Upgrade Actually Matter?
item.description:
If you’ve ever spec’d a part that needs to be strong, tough, and heat-treatable, you’ve probably landed on 4140 or 4340. They’re two of the most popular alloy steels in manufacturing, and on paper they look similar. Both are chromium-molybdenum steels. Both machine well. Both respond to heat treatment.

So why does 4340 cost more, and when does it actually matter?

The Quick Difference

The short answer is nickel. 4340 contains approximately 1.65-2.00% nickel, while 4140 has none. That nickel addition gives 4340 better toughness, deeper hardenability, and higher fatigue resistance — especially in larger cross-sections.

item.title: The 5 Families of Stainless Steel (And How to Tell Them Apart)
item.description:
“Stainless steel” isn’t one material. It’s a family of at least five distinct types, each with different properties, different strengths, and different use cases. Lumping them together is like saying “wood” without distinguishing between balsa and oak.

Here’s how the five families break down and why it matters for your next project.

1. Austenitic

This is what most people think of when they hear “stainless steel.” The 300-series grades — 304, 316, 321 — are all austenitic. They’re non-magnetic, highly corrosion-resistant, and very formable. They can’t be hardened by heat treatment, only by cold working.

item.title: About
item.description:
Steel is the most used metal on the planet. It holds up your buildings, moves your car, and runs through the infrastructure you never think about. And yet, most people — even people who work with it — carry around a head full of half-truths about how it actually works.

This site exists to fix that.

Steel AF is a blog for anyone who’s ever wondered why stainless steel still stains, why sparks fly off a grinder, or why two pieces of the “same” steel can behave completely differently. The answers are more interesting than you’d expect.
